name: Synthetic Response Generation Methods Baseline
summary: The first staged generation-methods baseline finds at least five distinct synthetic response generation families: rubric- and profile-conditioned LLM generation, generative-agent learner simulation, example- and error-conditioned prompt generation, non-LLM corruption-based generation, and prompt-strategy-based simulated essay generation.
main_blockers: The pass is targeted rather than systematic.
Some method details remain at repository or abstract level rather than full prompt-template level.
Included sources span mathematics proofs, personalized learning, scientific argumentation chatbot inputs, and essay scoring, so task-level generalization is limited.
best_current_signals: Ipci et al. define knowledge-profile and representative-example LLM generation strategies for rubric-aligned responses.
Agent4Edu defines a learner-profile, memory, reflection, and action-module architecture for response generation by generative agents.
Watts et al. generate synthetic student inputs through Copilot prompts conditioned on high-quality, low-quality, and typo examples.
Yoo et al. provide a non-LLM CASE corruption strategy for rubric-aligned essay training samples.
Wolfe et al. compare prompt strategies for simulated essay generation from real essays.
interpretive_note: This baseline maps generation mechanisms, not universal best practices. Generation methods differ in what they condition on: profiles, examples, target quality, errors, memory state, real essay continuations, or procedural corruption.
limitations: The staged sources are a compact bootstrap set, not an exhaustive methods review.
The included methods use different tasks, response formats, and evaluation goals.
Generation model versions and exact prompt templates are not consistently reported across sources.
This claim does not establish that any generation method produces valid replacement data for scorer validation or classroom inference.
update_notes: Run a broader database search before treating this as a complete methods section.
Separate prompt-engineering effects, model-generation effects, quality evaluation, and real-response-comparison claims in later bundles.

supporting_evidence.label: Profile- and rubric-conditioned LLM generation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Knowledge profiles and representative examples are one direct family of synthetic student response generation methods.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Ipci et al. describe LLM generation guided by knowledge profiles and representative examples, including straightforward and error-informed strategies.
limitations: The staged extraction does not include full prompt templates.
The source is bounded to discrete mathematics logical-proof responses.

supporting_evidence.label: Generative-agent learner simulation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Learner-profile, memory, reflection, and action-module agents are a distinct response-generation architecture.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Agent4Edu describes LLM-powered generative agents equipped with learner profile, memory, and action modules for response generation.
limitations: The finding is architecture-level unless full implementation details are reviewed.
Simulator responses are not necessarily rubric-labeled assessment answers.

supporting_evidence.label: Example-, quality-, and error-conditioned prompting
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic inputs can be generated by conditioning prompts on high-quality, low-quality, and error-injected examples.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Watts et al. report Copilot prompt sets that use high-quality examples, low-quality examples, and typographical-error conditioning.
limitations: The generated responses are chatbot inputs for prototype evaluation.
The method is not an automated scorer validation dataset.

supporting_evidence.label: Corruption and prompt-strategy boundaries
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Generation methods are not all LLM prompt generation; corruption-based and prompt-strategy methods should be classified separately.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Yoo et al. use non-LLM CASE corruption, while Wolfe et al. compare multiple prompt strategies for simulated essay generation.
limitations: Wolfe et al. is a preprint and model-version details are not staged.

applies_when: The question is which generation mechanisms have appeared in the reviewed literature.
does_not_apply_when: The question is whether those mechanisms are valid replacements for real scored responses.
unknown_or_unreviewed_contexts: Full prompt-template coverage and paywalled or non-indexed generation studies.
